title: Roadmap: The Swaraj Stack - Indian resilience in a connected world
gist: Bessemer lays out the 'Swaraj Stack' investment thesis: India must domestically build six layers of AI infrastructure (energy, compute, data, models, infra software, applications/physical AI) plus cybersecurity, because recent chip/energy/border shocks exposed foreign dependency across compute, energy and security. Regulation and government tenders demanding data residency are creating a multi-billion-dollar sovereign-infrastructure demand floor.

insights.claim: India's Aadhaar biometric ID system, built from vendor-neutral open-source parts and commodity sensors rather than a turnkey purchase, now runs at 1.4 billion enrollments, demonstrating that national-scale technical infrastructure can be assembled domestically without excluding global tech.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Sets the template investors should expect for AI-stack buildouts: open, domestically-assembled infrastructure at population scale rather than imported turnkey systems.
insights.quote: Aadhaar, India’s biometric ID system, was assembled from vendor neutral, open source parts and tech; commodity sensors, open-source software, affordable servers, and designed from start to finish in India.

insights.claim: India's UPI payments protocol, built atop Aadhaar as a public rail open to every bank and app, now carries nearly half the world's real-time payments by volume and processes over 22 billion transactions a month.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Proves India can produce globally dominant infrastructure via open public protocols; the same model is being extended to AI rails.
insights.quote: UPI now carries nearly half the world's real-time payments by volume
insights.locator: The results: ~50 years of financial inclusion

insights.claim: According to the World Bank, India's digital public infrastructure compressed roughly 50 years of financial inclusion into 6 years and saved over $30B in welfare leakage.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Quantifies the economic upside of sovereign digital rails, underwriting the thesis that AI rails deliver comparable step-change value.
insights.quote: ~50 years of financial inclusion compressed into 6, per the World Bank; over $30B in welfare leakage saved

themes.theme: Geopolitical stress test and sovereignty imperative

insights.claim: The Hormuz crisis sent Brent crude past $120, exposing that India imports over 85% of its crude oil requirements and that shocks hit layers of the value chain India depends on but does not own: compute, energy and security.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Frames energy import dependency as an acute macro vulnerability that motivates domestic buildout across the AI stack.
insights.quote: The Hormuz crisis sent Brent past $120, reminding India that it imports over 85% of its crude oil requirements.
insights.locator: The past year was a stress test

insights.claim: A made-in-India application or robot built on imported compute, data and models would export margin to whoever owns the foundational layers, meaning the economic durability of top-of-stack products depends entirely on owning the layers beneath.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Margin capture accrues to owners of the foundational layers; investors should weight compute/data/model plays over pure app plays for defensibility.
insights.quote: A made-in-India application or robot built on imported compute, data, and models would export margin to whoever owns the foundational layers.
insights.locator: The economic value at this layer

insights.claim: Bessemer's 'Swaraj Stack' thesis explicitly rejects isolationism, positioning India's AI buildout as mastery of domestic capabilities while remaining dependent on global trade, capital and talent, with named portfolio bets including Sarvam, Mitigata, Seekho, Inrisk and Protectt.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Signals where a major VC is deploying; these portfolio names map to the layers Bessemer believes will compound over the coming decade.
insights.quote: Swaraj (literally self-governance in Sanskrit), to us means mastery of one’s capabilities while learning from the world's.
insights.locator: We call this the Swaraj Stack

insights.claim: India's installed power generation capacity crossed 520 GW in early 2026 and is projected to roughly double to 1,121 GW by 2035-36 just to meet base demand, requiring a 600 GW addition dominated by 509 GW of solar and 155 GW of wind.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: A once-in-a-generation energy buildout underpins every downstream AI opportunity; solar and wind supply chains face a decade of demand.
insights.quote: projects it to roughly double to 1,121 GW by 2035-36 simply to meet base demand

insights.claim: Datacenters consume roughly 0.5% of India's electricity today but are on a path to 3% by 2030 as installed datacenter capacity rises from 1.5 GW to 4.5-9 GW, with a single hyperscale AI cluster pulling 100 MW or more continuously.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: A 6x jump in datacenter power share signals intense demand for continuous stable supply, storage and grid intelligence.
insights.quote: A hyperscale AI cluster pulls 100 MW or more continuously - the largest such as xAI’s Colossus 2 draw more than a gigawatt.

insights.claim: India's grid will need 174 GW of dedicated storage by 2035-36 (80 GW batteries and 94 GW pumped hydro) to absorb its solar-heavy generation mix, a requirement worsened by AI's continuous 24-hour load profile.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Grid-scale storage (batteries and pumped hydro) plus grid-intelligence software are direct investable beneficiaries of the solar-plus-AI load mismatch.
insights.quote: the grid needs 174 GW of dedicated storage by 2035-36, 80 GW of batteries and 94 GW of pumped hydro, to absorb the solar-heavy generation mix
insights.locator: The implication is a once-in-a-generation build

insights.claim: Meeting AI's continuous load will require pairing utility-scale solar with battery and pumped-hydro storage, gas peakers for ramp, and eventually small modular nuclear reactors for steady baseload.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: SMRs and gas peakers are flagged as future baseload/ramp solutions, pointing to longer-dated energy investment themes beyond renewables.
insights.locator: For instance, utility-scale solar will need to be paired

insights.claim: Colliers projects $20-25B of additional datacenter capex flowing into India by 2030, while S&P tracks more than $32B in datacenter investment announcements over the last two years.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Sizes the near-term datacenter opportunity across developers, power/cooling, operators and colocation platforms.
insights.quote: Colliers projects $20-25B of additional datacenter capex flowing into India by 2030

insights.claim: India's compute layer is heavily exposed to foreign supply chains: GPU silicon and networking fabric are designed and fabricated outside India, and semiconductor imports crossed $20B in FY24 alone, up 18.5% year-on-year, with monolithic IC imports up 2,000% since FY16.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Quantifies the import-dependency gap that domestic fabs, custom silicon and neo-clouds are being funded to close.
insights.quote: India's semiconductor imports crossed $20B in FY24 alone, up 18.5% year-on-year, with monolithic IC imports alone up 2,000% since FY16.
insights.locator: India’s compute layer has significant exposure

insights.claim: India Semiconductor Mission 2.0 commits $13B in incentives over 12 years to build out fabs, assembly, packaging and full-stack chip design, and Deloitte projects India's semiconductor market could reach $120B by 2030 from $45B today.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: State-backed capital plus a ~2.7x market expansion frames semiconductors as a policy-underwritten growth vector through 2030.
insights.quote: Deloitte projects that India's overall semiconductor market could reach $120B by 2030 from $45B today.
insights.locator: To spur India-based innovation in semiconductors

insights.claim: Government tenders in defence, public sector banks and state cloud now constitute a multi-billion-dollar domestic compute demand floor that did not exist three years ago, expected to trigger innovation in neo-clouds, custom compute, interconnect silicon and orchestration software.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Guaranteed government demand de-risks domestic compute startups; watch neo-cloud and custom-silicon entrants targeting state tenders.
insights.quote: Government tenders alone in defence, public sector banks, state cloud are a multi-billion-dollar demand floor that simply did not exist three years ago.
insights.locator: We believe that India’s local inference demands

insights.claim: India hosts one of the largest sets of population-scale, consent-anchored datasets across 22 official languages, with UPI processing 22 billion+ transactions monthly and Ayushman Bharat Health Account exceeding 850 million health IDs.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: India's structural data advantage scales super-linearly with population, creating a durable moat for locally-trained AI.
insights.quote: A country’s digital data generation scales super-linearly with the population size - this is where India has a massive advantage.

insights.claim: Indic languages make up only about 1% of the Common Crawl corpora frontier models train on and Indic text on the open web is closer to 0.1% (versus 59% for English), despite India being 18% of the world's population.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: The stark data-representation gap is the core justification for sovereign, locally-pretrained models and Indic data-refinement businesses.
insights.quote: Indic languages are about 1% of the Common Crawl corpora frontier models train on; India is 18% of the world's population.

insights.claim: The global data labeling and annotation market was roughly $3.8B in 2024 heading to $17B by 2030, with video annotation growing fastest at 23%+ CAGR, and India holds a structural advantage in physical/egocentric AI data given its manufacturing, farm environments and English-speaking annotators.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Data annotation (especially video/physical AI) is flagged as an export-driver where winners compete on accuracy over price.
insights.quote: The global market for data labeling and annotation was roughly $3.8B in 2024, heading to $17B by 2030, with video annotation growing fastest at 23%+ CAGR.

insights.claim: AIKosh, an open-source multilingual platform launched under the IndiaAI Mission in 2025, crossed 5,500+ datasets and 250+ models by December 2025, creating scaffolding for entrepreneurs to build monetisable 'Bharat Data Commons' layers.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Points to a concrete government data platform on which indigenous, monetisable data-refinement startups can be built.
insights.quote: crossed 5,500+ datasets and 250+ models by December 2025

insights.claim: India does not need to build frontier-class models from scratch; the winning model layer is a different race defined by architectures that are multilingual by default, voice-native, and tuned for reasoning over Indian context.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Reframes the India model opportunity away from a losing GPU-scale race toward differentiated multilingual/voice-native models.
insights.quote: We don’t believe that the model layer in India is a copy of the global foundation model race, but rather a different race.
insights.locator: This doesn’t mean that India needs to develop frontier-class

insights.claim: Nation-critical inference workloads will need to be served by sovereign models that are owned, pre-trained and run domestically, within a hybrid market that also uses global frontier and global open-source models.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Regulated and national-security inference is a protected market for domestic model builders, insulated from global model competition.
insights.quote: Nation-critical inference workloads will need to be served by sovereign models that are owned, pre-trained, and run domestically.
insights.locator: We believe that India will be a hybrid market

insights.claim: India's banking and investment services sector alone spent $15B on IT in 2025, up from $13.2B in 2024 per Gartner, with AI inference the fastest-growing component.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Sizes a large, growing enterprise spend pool where AI inference demand is accelerating fastest.
insights.quote: India's banking and investment services sector alone spent $15B on IT in 2025, up from $13.2B in 2024 per Gartner, with AI inference growing fastest.

insights.claim: Of 78 million MSMEs registered on India's Udyam portal (up from 41 million two years ago), most remain undigitized, and AI-native document intelligence is emerging as the layer that finally makes large-scale digitization economical for them and for thousands of enterprises above $50M revenue still running on legacy systems.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: A vast undigitized SMB and mid-market base is the addressable wedge for AI-native document/compliance automation.
insights.quote: AI-native document intelligence is emerging as the layer that finally makes large-scale digitization economical for this segment.

insights.claim: India's indigenous defence production hit a record ~$18B in FY25, up 18%, with procurement policy explicitly favouring domestic systems, positioning AI-led defence tech such as drones and autonomous surveillance robots for significant outcomes.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Record defence spend with domestic-preference procurement creates a protected demand pull for physical/embodied AI startups.
insights.quote: Indigenous production hit a record ~$18B in FY25, up 18%, with procurement policy explicitly favouring domestic systems.
insights.locator: Physical AI is the other imminent wave

insights.claim: The AI era introduces new attack surfaces including prompt injection, model theft, training-data poisoning, agent hijacking and deepfake fraud, and India faces an acute cybersecurity talent shortage where the nascent AI stack is 'one breach away from becoming someone else's leverage.'
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Indigenous cybersecurity spanning OT/energy to hardened agents is a cross-cutting opportunity; Bessemer already backs Mitigata and Protectt here.
insights.quote: the nascent AI stack is one breach away from becoming someone else’s leverage

insights.claim: A stack of Indian policies including a new $10B RDI fund, the IndiaAI Mission's $1.2B outlay, the India Semiconductor Mission 2.0's $13B, the DPDP Act and RBI's FREE-AI framework are converting regulatory data-residency and localisation requirements into direct demand for sovereign infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Regulation itself manufactures demand: every compliance mandate and data-residency tender is a customer for domestic-stack vendors.
insights.quote: Every enterprise that has to comply, every government tender that demands data residency, every regulated workflow that cannot legally run on offshore inference is demand creation for the layers in the stack.

insights.claim: Bessemer scores nine sectors on national security exposure, data sensitivity, cultural/linguistic dependency and economic leverage risk, ranking Defence, BFSI, Healthcare and Space as the top four where the Swaraj Stack will see highest adoption over the next decade.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Directs capital toward Defence, BFSI, Healthcare and Space as the highest-urgency verticals for indigenous AI solutions.
insights.quote: The top four sectors, Defence, BFSI, Healthcare and Space, represent the frontier of where we believe the Swaraj Stack will have highest adoption over the next decade.

insights.claim: With Skyroot Aerospace's successful Vikram-1 launch, India became the third country in the world after the US and China to own a domestic orbital launch capability, opening opportunities in space transportation, infrastructure, satellites and AI-powered services.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: A concrete milestone signaling a nascent Indian private space sector with classified-imagery and national-security demand drivers.
insights.quote: India became the third country in the world (after the US and China) to own a domestic orbital launch capability

insights.claim: Across the Swaraj Stack, digital assets (data, models, applications) can be built in months while physical assets (energy, compute, robotics) take years, implying divergent capital requirements and timelines for the category-defining companies of India's next decade.
insights.speaker: Priya Kale
insights.so_what: Investors should match capital structure and hold periods to layer physics: fast-compounding software vs. long-cycle physical infrastructure.
insights.quote: data, models and applications are digital assets that can be built in months, while energy, compute and robotics are physical assets that take years to develop
insights.locator: Each layer in the Swaraj Stack has distinct character
